meta.title: This House believes that Solana culture is cooked.

transcript:
Brian Smith: Hey guys. Thanks for joining us after lunch. Appreciate you taking the time to learn why Solana culture is firmly cooked. So, I've been in Solana since early 2021, full-time for over four years now, and I've seen the culture dynamics a lot over that time period. And it's pretty clear things are in a different place today. And so, early days, 2021, Solana was nothing. It was just a dream, right? It was like, is there any chance that we can possibly overcome Ethereum? Ethereum was everything in crypto smart contract world then. This was firmly the era of chewing glass. Tools on Solana sucked. Chain was breaking all the time. Open source project came around called Anchor. All the devs in the community pushing to that, try and create tooling to like get some product out the door. And so, there was the world, the Ethereum community, just against Solana, and we were trying to survive and make a name for ourselves and prove that there's actually a possible vision here. And any product that came out was just celebrated by everyone because we needed some real products on Solana mainnet that were halfway decent. Whether it was Phantom, which revolutionized crypto, whether it was Mango, which had the ICO, had the first real perpetual decentralized perpetual DEX. Like, all of these products, everyone just rejoiced and took part in. And this actually really continued through the 2022 period. Solana price went up, went down, new people entered the ecosystem, but it was still just chewing glass, working through all the issues, getting stuff out the door. Then at the end of 2022, we had FTX collapse. And this just reinforced the pure monoculture of Solana trying to survive, get shit done, and make a name for the community.

Eden Vander Zee: How did the FTX collapse reinforce a monoculture on Solana?

Brian Smith: Because it was, we need to ship or die, pretty much. Like, we are going to collapse. We were the... Sorry, how long do I respond to these? Sorry? Do I keep talking to your question or do I go? Yeah. Okay. Well, yeah. Anyways, so, um, like, it's us against the world was the time then. And it's not the time at all now. Is it Solana against the world? No, Solana is doing quite well. So anyways, back to it. FTX reinforced all of that. And it was 75 devs, the famous meme. The monoculture was just, we need to get out there, we need to find users. Bonk was like the personification of this at the time actually. Just sign up for a Discord and you get tens of thousands of dollars. Everyone in Solana was just cheerleading Bonk for a year because it was reinjecting capital and users into the ecosystem. And it worked. And now Solana is nowhere close to a monoculture. It is hundreds, thousands of subcultures that have very little overlap besides just the basics of existing in a human society. And so, think about it when we see it today. It's like, I'm part of the Jito community. It is a great community with different cultural values. Compare that to the Jupiter community. Also a great community, but they are extremely different. It is hard to say there's like shared culture between those two.

Ramzy Ali: Are you saying that Jito community, Jupiter community, any of these communities on Solana, are you saying that they don't collaborate with each other? Or are you just saying that it's a lack of similar interests?

Brian Smith: There's like technical collaboration, but I don't think there is significant cultural overlap between like Catstonbull and what we do at Jito meetups. Um, and so, like you can see this on Breakpoint. Like, you have people here in suits talking about institutional stuff and all of that, and yet you have the huge Bonk dog outside and the memecoin shillers and the streamers and all of that. And like, there is no overlap. They are not talking in the halls. These are completely separate cultures trying to do completely separate things with their products. And so, it's across the board. The Solana Chinese community has very, very little in common with like the New York builder scene. It is just people accomplishing their own goals. They all have their own values. And that's healthy as an ecosystem evolves. There was an early internet culture that was around kind of posting and all of this stuff. There's like no internet culture today. It's all defined by like individual subprojects. And I think it's also even more true when you contrast it with other crypto networks. Bitcoin has a monolithic culture. It is 21 million, digital gold, ossification of the protocol. Ethereum has this core decentralization ethos that defines their culture. Avalanche, um, Hyperliquid, Base. All of those you can clearly define what actually is included in that culture. And none of this applies to Solana because we've been so successful and just fragmented across everything and any of the original unifying cultures are gone now. And so that is why the House firmly believes Solana culture is cooked.

Host: Let's get a round of applause for that. Good stuff. Good job.

Pedro Miranda: Hello everyone. It's a privilege to be here. My name is Pedro Miranda. I formally run the consumer team at the Solana Foundation, or as Ramzy likes to say, I run all things fun at the Solana Foundation and he does all things boring. And that is also indicative of a really vibrant culture that we actually have here at Solana Foundation. Um, first, thank you to all the close to 7,000 attendees of Breakpoint across a diversity of audiences as Brian mentioned, uh, who are here to celebrate Solana ecosystem culture and learn about the different activations here today, and to all the new folks here. This is actually my fifth Breakpoint and my third as a Solana Foundation member. Uh, I actually had the privilege of joining the Solana Foundation actually a week after FTX collapsed. And it was the decision that so many Solana builders made to double down on the chain, uh, so many smart people from Tensor, from Helius, Magic Eden, etc., to double down on a chain that seemingly had nothing, but had everything pertaining to its technological and cultural future. And that made me want to take a bet on this community, uh, despite the shortcomings that it was coming to. So, we were asked to evaluate the following proposition of Solana culture being cooked. In other words, Brian and Ramzy think that these ideas and customs and social behaviors of the Solana community have degraded to a point where it can't sustain innovation, uh, or vibrant participation. And we think this claim is categorically false. I've had, you know, three examples of this on the, on the tech side, on the tech and product side. First being that, you know, competition and cooperation is the...

Brian Smith: Objection. Did we claim that subcultures are orthogonal to growth and innovation?

Pedro Miranda: You were claiming that subculture... This is actually my question to Brian that I actually was hinting at, as the fact that they have subcultures with different interests does not mean that you cannot cooperate with one another. Actually, Ramzy is the best example of this in terms of being able to, to hit different ends of the spectrum. Ramzy is an amazing, uh, resident degen we call him at the Solana Foundation. He's actually a great user of all the top DeFi protocols on Solana as well as consumer apps. But you should also see him suit up and go and pitch an institution on why to build on Solana. And that's actually the foundation of how these different interests actually converge on one single global network. Ramzy is a power user of all these applications that have generated, uh, close to $1.5 billion in, uh, revenue themselves this past year to date. And he's able to take those numbers and go into, go on to these institutions to actually convince why Solana is the best place to build their financial products. And that's also led to the JP Morgans of the world, Western Union, to actually choose Solana as the home for natively for many of their products. Uh, and it's all the foundation of fun, right? Um, for example, you know, one of the examples that I wanted to mention is that the, this competition and cooperation is the basis for a lot of the network today. For example, in September 2021, Brian, you were mentioning the early days, uh, I previously was an NFT protocol called Metaplex, and the NFT mints were consistently taking the network down. They were battle testing, they were a cause, they were hemorrhaging the network. And a lot of non-NFT apps or DeFi protocols were saying, hey, can't we just blocklist the Metaplex smart contracts? Can't we just not allow this NFT minting contract to take, consistently take down the network? And Solana and the validator community actually said no. We're actually going to come to a conclusion, we're actually going to stress test the network and find a solution for all this net new activity, and in some cases spam, that is occurring as a result of all these NFT mints during Solana Summer in 2021. So that is an example of the different interests that might not seem aligned actually come to a net positive, uh, conclusion for the Solana network. And ultimately, I think that's indicative of how Solana culture has not only been battle tested, but it's indicative of how Solana is... you have strong opinions that are loosely held. And anyone can go on Crypto Twitter and have access to totally any of the top protocol engineers to make a pitch and make a change. And that's why I think Solana is for everyone. So, I appreciate the time guys.

Ramzy Ali: Okay. Uh, compliments aside and thank you Pedro, I do appreciate them. Um, I want to start off by saying that, uh, this is indeed my first Breakpoint at the Solana Foundation. Uh, in terms of seniority on the stage here, of time in and on Solana, I am by far the most junior. And so I think I am uniquely positioned to speak to Solana's culture today. Um, I would argue that, uh, fundamentally Solana's, uh, culture, and when you think about culture as a broader concept, uh, you have to, uh, break down what that means, right? And so I think that Solana culture is, uh, is fundamentally representative of many subcultures. And Brian has indicated this in his speech. Um, but what I want to talk about today is how, uh, some of these subcultures have resulted in, um, infighting and potentially some, uh, not so great outcomes on, on the timeline as well as, uh, to the overall growth of the ecosystem. Um, subcultures are, uh, inherently competitive. Uh, one team is, uh, trying to compete for liquidity or mindshare with the other. Uh, we saw this with, uh, Jito and Harmonic recently, not to put you on, on the spot Brian. Uh, we saw this with, uh, Jupiter and Titan and DFlow. We even saw this, uh, Eden with you and getting into some, some Twitter beef on, on your end. And I think that fundamentally when we think about onboarding the net new user, they become somewhat confused, uh, when they look on Twitter and they see all of us fighting each other, uh, for small wins and market share as opposed to, uh, working collectively like we did in 2021 to grow the network out of stagnation out and, and actually innovate. Um, this, uh, this notion needs to be revived. Um, Brian and I both believe that subcultures can work together. Um, but the, the House today is here to argue to you that Solana culture is cooked in its current form. That's not to say that we can't improve it. Um, subcultures are really important because they allow each of, um, our fantastic app developers, our founders, our communities to establish their own language, to establish their own, um, their own shared beliefs, their shared identities, and to move forward and grow their own ecosystems. Um, this is really important as we expand the number of use cases on Solana. Solana is not just made for one use case. It's a fantastic trading chain. It's also great for, um, community stuff and, and all of the consumer content that Pedro focuses on like, uh, memecoins, NFTs, uh, as well as DePIN, AI. All of these products require a, uh, very clear culture. Um, if we were to look at, uh, Web2, and I don't want to do this too often at Breakpoint, but if we look at our Web2 counterpart, um, all of the applications don't share a unified culture because they're on the internet. Uh, they have their own cultures. Apple has a culture, Instagram has a culture, Meta, whatever you want to call it. Um, all of these products, all of these teams have a unique identity. And this is required if we want to grow past just an experimental chain. Um, Solana is doing incredible stuff. We're, we're hitting, um, record after record. I spoke about this, um, a couple days ago. There's, uh, there's, there's immense activity and you can feel the potential energy of Solana in real time. Um, but in order for us to move forward and to have, uh, collective growth as an ecosystem, these subcultures need to understand that PVP on the timeline does not look good and represents quite poorly on the, on the aggregate. Um, institutions have come to me many times, and as Pedro mentioned, I, I like to spend my time in between both camps. Institutions have come to me many times and have asked why are both of the routers, or all three of the routers on your chain arguing with each other on Twitter? I think that's, uh, that's, uh, it's a pretty poor representation of what, uh, Solana could be. And so I think, uh, in, in summary, in order for us to grow, uh, we have to understand that these applications, these communities, these teams are going to need their own subcultures. And therefore Solana as a collective culture should not really exist. It should be a fabric, a rich tapestry of many different subcultures. Um, and, uh, in addition, um, when these cultures evolve, there's growing pains. And there's going to be, uh, PVP. Uh, but that's okay. And we need to, we need to address it and move forward and grow collectively as we did back in 2020, uh, 2022. And, um, yeah, I think this is, uh, this is, this is all I have today. Thank you.

Eden Vander Zee: Ramzy, would you like to join this side of the stage? Because I think you're, uh, you're on our side, um, based off your discussion, but appreciate that. To the members of the Solana community here and online, I stand here today representing you. I'm on my politician arc, uh, today too. So, here we go. Um, we are against the motion that culture is cooked on Solana, um, for a few different very specific reasons. Um, I've been in the Solana community long enough to remember when it was literally 75 devs in a hacker house and about eight women collectively across the community. Um, and maybe a few Discord channels too. Uh, the vibes were friendly and welcoming and warm, um, which honestly drew me to the ecosystem initially. That feeling I believe is still here, um, and we've been growing up along the way. So, now we kind of have institutional eyes here. We've had, uh, we've built incredible products, um, and there's more users entering the ecosystem every day. So when I hear Solana culture is cooked, I can't help but look around at 7,000 people that have traveled around the world from 120 countries to celebrate the incredible products that are being built on Solana, um, and represent a, a strong community and culture, um, that exists here today. Despite whatever happens on Crypto Twitter, which I agree, um, we could, we could do much better in the toxicity levels that exist there. Um, but you look at the volume of community members that actually show up to support Solana and represent, uh, everything we're building here. We believe that Solana's culture has played a critical role in the momentum of this ecosystem, which inherently proves its undeniable and fruitful existence.

Brian Smith: Do you think the monopolistic tendencies of, uh, some of the teams on Solana contribute to a positive culture?

Eden Vander Zee: Absolutely not. Um, I would think anyone that wants to build a monopoly could understand why that would be problematic for a diverse ecosystem. People care deeply. Um, it's why we're having this debate right now. Um, as you can see, this is pretty much proof of culture right here. If no one cared about culture, we wouldn't be having a debate on if it's cooked or not. Um, that, this is a clear indication that there is culture. So, um, when I hear the words again like Solana culture is cooked, it's, I hear frustration with the, with change, right? Because we have changed a lot from, you know, degen NFT mints and like early vibes to a little bit more serious. Um, we're, we're growing up as a community, uh, which is good. It brings, we have institutional eyes on us, um, and that comes with a little bit of, um, uncomfort, uh, but it's not evidence of a cultural collapse. Um, so what's happening here is not cultural death. I think it's a bit dramatic, even as a woman. Um, I think it's kind of funny to say that it's cooked completely. Um, we're just growing up, we're starting to feel it. Uh, and so yeah, we've, it's very clear that we've, you know, graduated past NFT mints, memecoins, and real products are kind of surfacing, um, as a result of that. The opposition is going to try to argue that growth does not represent culture. Um, but I would say that it is an indicator of culture. Because without a strong community, no protocol, blockchain, business, or application can truly stand the test of time. I think if you look at blockchains and compare them one to one, um, Solana has withstood a lot. Uh, just think back to the opening video that was played at the start of this conference. Um, we kind of saw, we saw this entire ecosystem fall, right? Very, very, very low. Um, we were, we were in tough times, but this community has prevailed and, um, come out on the other side. So, uh, that to me is the biggest indicator of a strong culture and community, um, that has withstood those, those tests. Um, the opposition also spoke to competition, right? Um, Solana is maturing. Um...

Brian Smith: Uh, just to go back a little bit. Do you, uh, believe or do you not believe that cultures can die and then become reborn?

Eden Vander Zee: I don't think that the Solana culture has died at any point. Um, but to answer your question personally, yes, I believe that a culture could, could die and then be reborn. But I don't think that's what's happened here. Um, I think it's changed, uh, and we have matured and we're, we're entering a new era of Solana that where people are taking us very seriously. Um, and so that comes again just with a little bit of uncomfort. Um, industries don't scale when everyone agrees. Uh, they scale, um, when we agree on how to disagree. Um, so yeah, thank you both Ramzy and Brian for participating here. Um, finally I'll say that, oh, I'm over time. Um. No, just finish up. It's all, it's okay. I will, I will step aside. Thank you.

Ramzy Ali: Agree with you. Everything you said. Yeah, you're exactly right. Um, I think, uh, I think we're at the end of the day all on the same page here. Um, that the culture has changed. There's been some difficulties along the way, but, um, we can create the strongest community here. Um, kind of proven out with the 7,000 attendees that are in this room right now. Um, and 25,000 more online. Uh, what began with two coffees and a beer, uh, turned into hacker houses and into years of glass chewing and problem solving has now become a thriving community of relentless builders and newly welcomed institutions and dedicated founders bringing new ideas. I wanted to refrain from personal comments, um, because I don't think it is productive. Um, but it is, it's important to call out, um, that there is a level of toxicity that does exist in this culture. Um, and it's namely on Twitter. Uh, I don't think it is, um, useful, uh, to spend our time kind of proliferating, um, toxic comments against each other. Um, it ultimately does not help this, uh, community grow. So, um, yeah, to the builders, founders, creators, and everyone in between, it's really up to us holding the keys of what this community will become. Uh, so let's stop getting distracted with, um, comments on X and Crypto Twitter and build the Nasdaq on chain. Mm. Amen. Amen.

Host: Are you not entertained? Oh my god. This was incredible, you guys. Thank you all so much for being here. The votes are finishing up right now. Make sure you scan that Slido QR code, uh, and get the, lock your votes in right now. Literally moments to go.

Eden Vander Zee: Just, just a reminder, um, we are on this side that we believe Solana culture is not cooked. So I know the prompt's a little confusing but...

Host: Eden, I believe the debate is over. No longer can your points be made. While I do respect your opinion because I'm on that team. Um, alright great. It's over. It's done. Do you guys have fun out there? Woo! Purple team. Congratulations. Truly beautiful work. We're going to take a minute, reset the stage. Go grab a coffee but make it fast because we have an incredible afternoon of product keynotes from some of the biggest names on Solana. Alright? See you all soon.

enrichment.generated_query: Solana This House believes that Solana culture is cooked. Breakpoint 2025 announcement

tags.impact_reason: A debate about Solana's culture, while potentially interesting to some, doesn't have a significant impact on the Solana ecosystem as a whole.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose fractured glass as the Semantic Anchor to directly represent the 'chewing glass' ethos mentioned repeatedly in the transcript. The single monolith shattering into diverse crystalline forms visually narrates the story's core theme: the death of a 'monoculture' and the rise of a fragmented, multi-faceted ecosystem that includes both chaotic traders and structured institutions. I included the text '100K TPS' diegetically etched onto the monolith's surface to represent the original, unified technical goal that the community was painfully striving for during its 'ship or die' phase.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on a deep fissure in the dark glass monolith. A hyper-detailed, perfectly geometric cyan crystal emerges from the jagged, raw edge of a fracture. The image highlights the stark contrast in textures—the rough, stressed break of the original 'chewing glass' material against the pristine, calculated structure of the new formation, with light catching the sharp edges of both.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro shot focusing on a deep fissure in the dark glass monolith, composed for a SQUARE aspect ratio (1:1). A hyper-detailed, perfectly geometric cyan crystal emerges from the jagged, raw edge of a fracture. The image highlights the stark contrast in textures—the rough, stressed break of the original 'chewing glass' material against the pristine, calculated structure of the new formation, with light catching the sharp edges of both. The composition emphasizes symmetry and central focus, with tight macro framing that fills the entire frame with texture. Style of Fractured Glass, Crystalline Growth, Shattered Monolith, with dramatic Volumetric Lighting. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), ultra-photorealistic, cinematic lighting.

editorial.headline: Solana Monoculture Dies as Institutional Adoption Accelerates
editorial.standfirst: The transition from a unified "chewing glass" ethos to fragmented, competitive subcultures signals a critical maturity phase for the network. While internal friction rises, the ecosystem’s ability to host both memecoin traders and global banks validates its architectural thesis.
key_takeaways: Solana applications have generated nearly $1.5 billion in revenue year-to-date, driving internal competition.
Traditional entities like **JP Morgan** and **Western Union** are actively integrating, creating a distinct "suit" subculture separate from the "degen" base.
Infrastructure rivalry has intensified, leading to public friction between major protocols like **Jito** and **Jupiter** and their competitors.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The "chewing glass" era of 2021—defined by a singular, defensive unity against Ethereum—is officially over. In its place, **Solana** has evolved into a complex ecosystem of orthogonal subcultures, a shift that signals the network has graduated from survival mode to scaling mode. The debate at Breakpoint 2025 highlights a critical reality for investors: a unified culture is necessary for survival, but a fragmented, competitive culture is required for mass adoption. The friction observed between the "suits" pitching **JP Morgan** and the "degens" shilling **Bonk** is not a bug; it is a feature of a permissionless network reaching critical mass.

This fragmentation has introduced a new dynamic of "PvP" (Player vs Player) competition among infrastructure providers. As liquidity deepens, the stakes for dominance have risen, leading to public clashes between teams like **Jito** and **Harmonic**, or **Jupiter** and its DEX counterparts. While the optics of infighting may confuse traditional institutions initially, this fierce competition drives product velocity. The network is no longer relying on a single narrative to sustain value; it is powered by distinct, revenue-generating fiefdoms that operate independently of the **Solana Foundation**'s direct oversight.

Ultimately, the "death" of the Solana monoculture validates the thesis that the chain can support diverse, conflicting use cases simultaneously. The ecosystem has moved beyond the post-FTX trauma response of "ship or die" to a sophisticated economy generating nearly $1.5 billion in application revenue year-to-date. For institutional allocators, the takeaway is clear: **Solana** is no longer a tight-knit hacker house, but a sprawling digital metropolis where corporate interests and crypto-native volatility coexist.
